label: Tehillim (Psalms) — Part 5 — Psalms 90–106
summary_plain: Psalms 90 through 106 open with the only psalm attributed to Moshe: YHWH, you have been our dwelling place in all generations. Before the mountains were brought forth, from everlasting to everlasting, you are El. Psalm 91 promises shelter under the wings of the Most High. Psalm 100 calls all the earth to serve YHWH with gladness. Psalm 103 declares YHWH's compassion: as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions. Psalm 104 is the great creation hymn — YHWH stretches out the heavens like a tent, makes the clouds his chariot, rides on the wings of the wind. Psalm 106 recounts the covenant history from Mitzrayim through the wilderness to the land.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads Psalms 90-106 from DSS witnesses and the Orit Ge'ez. Psalm 90 is attributed to Moshe — the only psalm with this attribution — connecting the Tehillim to the Torah itself. Psalm 104 has long been compared to the Egyptian Great Hymn to the Aten (c. 1350 BCE), attributed to Akhenaten. Both texts praise the creator for the orderly provision of the natural world in strikingly parallel language. This is not borrowing but shared inheritance — both emerge from the same Afro-Asiatic intellectual tradition. The ACR places these texts in conversation. Psalm 104 is Hebrew praise; the Hymn to the Aten is Egyptian praise. Both come from the African continent's monotheistic impulse. The DSS fragments preserve variant readings in Psalm 104 and throughout this section that demonstrate the Masoretic is not the original form.

key_terms.definition: Psalm 90's superscription — 'A prayer of Moshe, the man of The Creator.' The only psalm attributed to Moshe. It establishes YHWH as the dwelling place of the covenant people across all generations.

Title used in Psalm 91:1 — 'He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High.' El Elyon is the supreme designation, placing YHWH above all other powers. The title appears in the DSS and in the Bereshit narrative of Malki-Tzedeq.

The Egyptian Great Hymn to the Aten (c. 1350 BCE), attributed to Akhenaten, shares striking parallels with Psalm 104 — both praise the creator for light, water, provision of food, and the orderly sustaining of life. Both emerge from the African monotheistic intellectual tradition.

fill_blank.prompt: YHWH, you have been our dwelling place in all ______. Before the mountains were brought forth, from everlasting to everlasting, you are El.
fill_blank.source_quote: YHWH, you have been our dwelling place in all generations. Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting, you are El.

fill_blank.prompt: He stretches out the heavens like a ______, he makes the clouds his chariot.
fill_blank.source_quote: Covering yourself with light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent.

fill_blank.prompt: As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our ______ from us.
fill_blank.source_quote: As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us.

multiple_choice.question: What is unique about Psalm 90?
options: It is the only psalm attributed to Moshe — a prayer of the man of The Creator
multiple_choice.source_quote: A prayer of Moshe, the man of The Creator. YHWH, you have been our dwelling place in all generations.

multiple_choice.question: What does Psalm 104 celebrate?
options: YHWH's creation — stretching out the heavens, making clouds his chariot, establishing the earth
multiple_choice.source_quote: Bless YHWH, O my soul! O YHWH my Creator, you are very great! You are clothed with splendor and majesty, covering yourself with light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent.

multiple_choice.question: What does Psalm 91 promise?
options: Shelter under the wings of the Most High — YHWH is my refuge and my fortress
multiple_choice.source_quote: He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty. I will say to YHWH: My refuge and my fortress, my Creator, in whom I trust.

faq.question: What is the connection between Psalm 104 and the Egyptian Hymn to the Aten?
faq.answer: Psalm 104 and the Egyptian Great Hymn to the Aten (c. 1350 BCE, attributed to Akhenaten) share striking structural and thematic parallels: light as the creator's garment, the provision of water and food for all creatures, the distinction of day and night, the dependence of all life on the creator's sustaining will. Western scholarship typically frames this as Hebrew 'borrowing' from Egypt — placing Egypt as the source and Israel as the derivative. The ACR framework rejects this hierarchy. Both texts emerge from the same Afro-Asiatic intellectual tradition — the African continent's monotheistic impulse. Moshe was 'learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians' (Acts 7:22). Hebrew and Egyptian are both Afro-Asiatic languages. The two traditions share a common ancestor. Psalm 104 is not borrowed — it is cognate. Two branches of the same African tree.

faq.question: Why does a psalm attributed to Moshe appear in the Psalter?
faq.answer: Psalm 90 is attributed to Moshe — the man of The Creator. This is the only psalm with this attribution. Its placement opens Book IV of the Psalter (Psalms 90-106), which follows the lament at the end of Book III (Psalm 89's cry that the Dawid covenant appears broken). The response to the apparent failure of the Dawid covenant is to go back further — to Moshe, to YHWH as the dwelling place of the covenant people in all generations, before the mountains, before the earth itself. The Great Eraser mechanism frequently disconnects Moshe from his African context — Moshe was raised in the Egyptian royal court, married a Cushite woman (Bemidbar 12:1), and operated in Northeast Africa for the first 80 years of his life. Psalm 90 connects the Torah and the Tehillim through the same African-rooted figure.
